content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
[ADDRESS AND CONTACT BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814]

August 23, 1977

A-77-367

Malinda Bergman
Frisco Bay Museal Group
54 Mint Street, 3rd Floor
San Francisco, California 94103

Dear Ms. Bergman:

The Fair Political Practices Commission has been a
copy of your full page advertisement labeled "STOP THE PERIPHERY CANAL" which appeared in the August 22,
1977, edition of the San Francisco Chronicle on page 11.
Your advertisement urged readers to contact certain state
and federal legislators in opposition to SB 346, which is
pending in the California legislature.

This is to advise you of certain of the requirements
of the Political Reform Act which may be applicable to
your group. In outlining the requirements, it is assumed
that the Frisco Museal Group does not employ a lobbyist,
since there is no registration on file with the Secretary
of State's Office. If a lobbyist is employed, there are
different requirements.

Under the Political Reform Act, any person who makes
payments of $250 or more in any month to influence legislative or administrative action must file reports on Form 650 pursuant to Government Code Sections 86108(b) and 86109.
Government Code Section 82015(c) defines such payments as
"[p]ayment for or in connection with soliciting or urging
other persons to enter into direct communication with any
elective state official, legislative official or agency
official." Accordingly the cost of developing and
publishing the advertisement which urges readers to contact
state officials is a payment to influence and the Frisco
Bay Museal Group has a filing obligation if these payments
exceed $250 or more in any one month.

Linda Bergman
Page Two                                                    August 23, 1977


Form 650 must be filed with the Secretary of State's
Office by the end of the month following the month in which
the expenditures were made. For your information, I am
enclosing a copy of Form 650 which should be filed by
September 30, 1977, reflecting any expenditures made in
August of 1977, which are payments to influence legislative
or administrative action. I am also enclosing a manual
which explains further the requirements for Government Code
Section 86101(b) filings on pages 4 and 6 through 10.

If you have any questions or there is any other infor-
mation which you need, please feel free to contact me at
(916) 32-4141.

                                                    Sincerely,

                                                    [signature]
                                                    Robert M. Stern
                                                    General Counsel

RMS:inc

Enclosures

HIMELSTEIN AND SAVINAR
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94113

MARK HIMELSTEIN
TIM SAVINAR

530 JACKSON ST., SUITE 201
PHONE: (415) 398-3574

October 4, 1977

Manager
Political Reform Division
Secretary of State
111 Capitol Mall
Sacramento, CA 94814

Dear Sir or Madam:

This office represents a party who paid for the full page
advertisement appearing in the August 22, 1977 edition of
the San Francisco Chronicle referred to in the letter from
Robert M. Stern, General Counsel of the Fair Political
Practices Commission, to Melinda Bergman, a copy of which
is enclosed herewith. Mr. Bergman is not the party who paid
for the advertisement. Nor was the Prisco Bay Mussel Group
the party paying for the advertisement, and finally, neither
the party nor the Prisco Bay Mussel Group has offices at
54 Mint Street, the address to which Mr. Stern directed his
correspondence.

In spite of all of the above, Mr. Stern's letter did reach
my client sometime in late September, and that party brought
the letter to me on September 30, 1977. On that day I
contacted Mr. Stern (though he did not return my call until
October 3). My client, the person paying for the ad, is not
a corporation nor is the party a lobbyist. The party is a
private person and is concerned with the fact that the party's
name will be disclosed to the public if form 650 is filed.
The party is considering a possible challenge to the appli-
cation of the act to this particular type of political
activity. Among other things, the party is concerned that
public disclosure of this type of political support may have
a chilling effect upon the exercise of political free speech
and advocacy.

My client will be out of town for the next two weeks, during
which time I intend to research the matter so that my client
can be advised thoroughly. For that reason, I am hereby re-
questing an extension of time to file the form, or, in the
alternative, a waiver of penalties in the event of fines
and a hereby extension of this to prepare a response
or to notify you of his intention to challenge the date. I
assure you that should my client elect to comply with the
act, the filing of your form 650 [illegible] with
all dispatch.

Manager, Political Reform Act [illegible]                                    Page 2


If you have any questions about any of the above, please
do not hesitate to contact me.

                                                Very truly yours,


                                                [signature]

THIS:ps

cc:  Robert M. Stern
     General Counsel
     Fair Political Practices Commission
     Box 807
     Sacramento, CA  95806

sections.question_synthetic: An attorney requests an extension of time to file Form 650 or a waiver of penalties for their client, a private individual who paid for a political advertisement, citing concerns about disclosure requirements and potential First Amendment implications.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains two distinct letters: an FPPC response letter followed by a follow-up correspondence from private counsel. The October 4 letter is the primary non-FPPC document being analyzed; it represents a request for administrative relief rather than an initial incoming request or formal withdrawal. Confidence slightly reduced due to the dual-document nature and lack of explicit section headers in the second letter.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: An attorney requests an extension of time to file Form 650 or a waiver of penalties for their client, a private individual who paid for a political advertisement, citing concerns about disclosure requirements and potential First Amendment implications.

embedding.summary: This document consists of two parts: (1) an August 23, 1977 FPPC advice letter from General Counsel Robert M. Stern to Melinda Bergman advising that the Frisco Bay Mussel Group must file Form 650 for expenditures on a political advertisement urging opposition to SB 346, and (2) an October 4, 1977 letter from an attorney to the Secretary of State's Political Reform Division correcting the record that neither Ms. Bergman nor the Frisco Bay Mussel Group paid for the advertisement, identifying their private client as the actual payor, and requesting an extension or penalty waiver to allow time for legal research regarding potential constitutional challenges.
